ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice food product class; no specific sambal fried rice with chicken wing and satay entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers fried rice as a category. No specific sambal fried rice with chicken wing and satay code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of sambal fried rice with one chicken wing and two to three chicken satay skewers, approximately 450 g total.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice) and Satay Ayam (Chicken Satay) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore Foodo composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values estimated by weighted combination of: (1) IMR MyFCD 'Nasi Goreng' (fried rice, Malaysian style) for the rice base; (2) HPB Singapore Foodo 'Satay Ayam' for the satay component; (3) HPB Singapore Foodo 'Chicken Wing (grilled/fried)' for the chicken wing component. Proportional weighting: rice base ~55%, chicken wing ~25%, satay ~20% of total dish weight. Sambal chilli paste contribution to sodium and fat incorporated from IMR MyFCD sambal data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — fried rice and mixed rice dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal fried rice with chicken wing and satay. GI of 54 estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base, Asian style), which reports GI values in the range 48–58. A midpoint of 54 is used, consistent with the mixed-ingredient composition that includes protein and fat from chicken and satay, which moderates glycemic response relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 96.75) / 100 ≈ 52.3, based on 450 g serving with 21.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal paste, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 2160 mg per 450 g serving), this dish provides a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sodium preparation, and avoid adding additional soy sauce or condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice base, partially offset by protein and fat from chicken and satay
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 54 (low category) and high glycemic load per full serving (~52) indicate that portion control is essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; consuming a half-portion with additional non-starchy vegetables and monitoring postprandial blood glucose is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from chicken skin and cooking oil, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 2.4 g saturated fat per 100 g and high sodium, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD should choose skinless chicken, request reduced-oil preparation, and limit serving frequency to occasional consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Fried Rice with Chicken Wing and Satay is a Malaysian-Singaporean Zi Char (煮炒) dish combining three distinct components on a single plate: a base of wok-fried rice cooked with sambal chilli paste, egg, shallots, garlic, and soy sauce; one or two grilled or deep-fried chicken wings; and two to three skewers of chicken satay served with peanut sauce. The fried rice is characterised by its bold, spicy, and slightly smoky flavour from the sambal paste, which is made from dried chillies, shrimp paste (belacan), shallots, and lemongrass. The chicken wing adds a crispy, savoury element, while the satay skewers contribute a sweet-savoury grilled flavour with aromatic spices including turmeric, coriander, and cumin. The dish is a hearty, calorie-dense meal typically served as a main course for lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein (~9.8 g per 100 g) from chicken and egg, moderate carbohydrate (~21.5 g per 100 g) from white rice, and moderate fat (~8.2 g per 100 g) from cooking oil and chicken. Sodium content is high (~480 mg per 100 g) due to sambal paste and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low category), moderated by the protein and fat content. This dish is commonly found at Zi Char stalls and Malaysian-Chinese restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia, and is popular as a satisfying one-plate meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sambal fried rice with chicken wing and satay?
What is the GI of fried rice?
Can diabetics eat sambal fried rice?
Is sambal fried rice high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of IMR MyFCD and HPB Foodo entries for the individual components (nasi goreng, chicken satay, grilled chicken wing). GI is estimated from the closest analogous fried rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are primary approved sources and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo source entries used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component source entries. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, and zinc are estimated from the weighted component sources and should be treated as approximate. The dish is classified as halal based on the typical preparation using chicken and plant-based ingredients without pork or alcohol; however, halal certification of the specific restaurant (Marina Seafood House - Boat Quay) has not been independently verified. Food group is reclassified from the input 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Mixed' to better reflect the composite nature of the dish (rice base + meat accompaniments). GI category is 'low' per the threshold rule (GI 54 ≤ 55 = low).

improvement_actions_taken: Reclassified food_group from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Mixed' to reflect composite dish nature.
Added mandatory multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean/Malaysian cuisine rules.
Constructed nutrient values via weighted composite estimation from IMR MyFCD and HPB Foodo approved sources.
Set GI to 54 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous fried rice entry; gi_category set to 'low' per ≤55 threshold rule.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes field per g3 sentinel documentation rule.

Corrected inconsistency in 'Nasi Goreng Sambal dengan Sayap Ayam dan Satay' (changed 'and' to 'dan') and added 'Sambal Nasi Goreng Ayam' to the flat aliases list to ensure consistency with aliases_by_language.
Set ontology match_type values to only allowed values: 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2, 'none' for SNOMED CT.
Ensured health_context includes Type 2 Diabetes with machine key 'type2_diabetes' and human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.
